message.content:
Fix REAL defects in a generated LLD session, then re-verify by running tests. Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/56b68468-1a94-427b-a556-a86f12dd402f_lld/

The adversarial reviewer found these actionable issues — fix EACH in the CODE and/or _response.md (keep them consistent — if you change code shown in a §6 ```python block, update that block too):
1. [BLOCKER] vending_service.py:79-86 (_lock hook) + 88-115 (public methods); design claims at vending_service.py:16-22, vending_service.py:101-105 (dispense docstring), _response.md:1019 (§9 Step 2): The headline concurrency angle is NOT implemented as claimed. The design repeatedly states 'the WHOLE insert→select→dispense transaction is atomic / one customer interacts at a time / only one customer's transaction runs at a time.' But the RLock is acquired and RELEASED inside each individual public call (insert_coin, select_product, dispense are three separate `with self._lock():` blocks). It is never held across the transaction. Because the machine is a SINGLE shared object, two interleaved customers share and corrupt the same `_inserted` list, `_selected_code`, and `_state`. I reproduced concrete money corruption deterministically (20/20 trials): Customer A inserts a NICKEL and pauses; Customer B inserts a DOLLAR (shared `_inserted` now = nickel+dollar = 105c), selects a 100c product, and dispenses — B walks away with 5c 'change' that is actually A's nickel, while A then gets InsufficientFundsError ('only 0 inserted') because their coin was consumed. This is exactly the invariant the problem demands be protected: 'concurrent button presses must not corrupt the state machine.' The lock makes each method atomic, not the transaction — so the claimed single-machine transactional lock does not exist.
   FIX: Either (a) hold one lock for the entire customer transaction — e.g. expose a `with svc.transaction():` context (or a single combined `purchase(coins, code)` API) that acquires the machine lock once around insert→select→dispense→cancel and have callers use it; or (b) explicitly scope the per-customer working memory so two customers cannot share `_inserted`/`_selected_code` (e.g. a session/transaction object keyed per caller, gated by the lock). Then update the docstrings/§9 text so the atomicity claim matches the code. Do not claim transaction-level atomicity while only providing call-level atomicity.
2. [MAJOR] tests/test_vending.py:201-249 (test_concurrent_buyers_race_for_last_unit): The barrier test does not prove the invariant under real contention; it passes trivially relative to the demanded scenario. Every thread buys the SAME product with an IDENTICAL coin (nickel), so the shared-`_inserted`-list corruption from the BLOCKER is masked (all contributions are interchangeable). I instrumented it over 200 trials: all 49 losers fail at `select_product` (the in-stock check), NEVER at `dispense`/`vend_one` — i.e. the 'race for the last unit at dispense' never actually races at dispense; the winner holds the lock through vend, and by the time losers acquire it stock is already 0 so they're rejected at select. The dispense-time check-then-act window the design dramatizes (§5.5 step 3 vs 7) is therefore never exercised by N>1 threads. The test also never asserts losers are REFUNDED, which the problem statement explicitly requires.
   FIX: Strengthen so contention is real and distinct: have threads insert different coin mixes and assert per-thread money conservation (total in == total dispensed-value + total refunded, no coin created/destroyed), and assert each loser's refund equals what they inserted. To actually force two threads into the dispense critical section concurrently, all N must pass `select` before any vend commits — inject the hold so multiple threads reach `vend_one` and assert the lock (not the early select reject) is what serializes them.
3. [MAJOR] tests/test_vending.py:252-319 (test_concurrent_buyers_with_hold_proves_lock_is_load_bearing), assertion at line 319 and comment at 316-318: The 'peak concurrency proof' is mislabeled and does not prove transaction atomicity. I counted hook invocations: `pre_vend_hook` fires EXACTLY ONCE (only the winner ever reaches `vend_one`; the other 29 are rejected at `select_product`, as the test's own comment admits at line 316-317). So `peak == 1` is achieved because only one thread ever enters the measured region — not because the lock serialized overlapping entrants. `peak` measures overlap inside `vend_one` only; it says nothing about whether the whole transaction is atomic (it is not — see BLOCKER). The claim at line 261 ('the lock makes the whole transaction atomic, so no two threads are ever inside the critical section') is therefore not what this assertion demonstrates. (Note: the unlocked base DOES drive peak=30/stock=-29, so the assertion has *some* teeth against vend_one overlap — but only because losers happen to lose at select under the lock, which is incidental to the stated proof.)
   FIX: Make the hold-test actually let multiple threads into `vend_one` simultaneously when unlocked (so the locked peak==1 is a genuine serialization proof of vend_one), and add a separate test that proves transaction-level atomicity (two interleaved DIFFERENT-product customers cannot cross-contaminate money/selection). Fix the comments to stop conflating vend_one-overlap with whole-transaction atomicity.

Rules: fix the CODE not the test (unless the test itself is the defect — e.g. too weak to prove the invariant — then strengthen it). Keep Python 3.11+ idioms, one domain exception base, no bare except, no '...' bodies, slow I/O outside locks. If you touch the §8 sequence diagram, ensure NO semicolons in message text. After fixing, run `cd /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/56b68468-1a94-427b-a556-a86f12dd402f_lld && /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python main.py` and `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q` and confirm both green. Update §7.1/§7.2 in _response.md if the test count/output changed. Return what you changed and final test status.

Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. - loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""
3	vending_service.py — the orchestrator (the "brain") + the thread-safe variant.
4	
5	VendingService is the public API a customer (or a UI) talks to. It owns the
6	machine's working memory — the coins inserted so far, the chosen slot, the
7	catalog of slots, the coin bank — and it FORWARDS each button press to the
8	current state object, then adopts whatever state that object returns.
9	
10	🎙️ "The service is deliberately thin. It does no business logic itself — it
11	    asks the current state to handle the action and swap itself out. That keeps
12	    the state-machine rules in the state classes where they belong. The service's
13	    only job is to hold the data and route the call."
14	
15	CONCURRENCY MODEL (one model, applied consistently):
16	    A single vending machine is ONE shared object that many button-press threads
17	    can hit at once. The whole insert -> select -> dispense -> cancel transaction
18	    must be atomic — one customer interacts at a time. So we guard the WHOLE
19	    machine with ONE lock (the machine IS the unit of contention; there is no
20	    per-slot parallelism to win — they share the inserted-coins tally and the
21	    coin bank). The clean core below has a no-op _lock() hook; the ThreadSafe
22	    subclass overrides ONLY that hook with a real RLock. §6/§7 stay lock-free.
23	"""
24	
25	import threading
26	from collections.abc import Iterator
27	from contextlib import contextmanager
28	
29	from models import (
30	    Coin,
31	    CoinBank,
32	    DispenseResult,
33	    IdleState,
34	    InvalidSelectionError,
35	    MachineState,
36	    MachineStateName,
37	    Slot,
38	    VendingContext,
39	)
40	
41	
42	class VendingService(VendingContext):
43	    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
44	    # The single entry point. It implements VendingContext so the State classes
45	    # can read/mutate its working memory without the service leaking its guts.
46	    # 🎙️ "Every customer action — insert, select, dispense, cancel — comes in
47	    #     here, gets wrapped in the (no-op) lock, and is delegated to the current
48	    #     state. The state returns the next state; we just store it."
49	
50	    def __init__(self, slots: dict[str, Slot], coin_bank: CoinBank) -> None:
51	        self._slots = slots
52	        self._coin_bank = coin_bank
53	        self._inserted: list[Coin] = []
54	        self._selected_code: str | None = None
55	        # The machine always boots in IDLE.
56	        self._state: MachineState = IdleState()
57	
58	    # ── VendingContext implementation (what the State classes see) ────────────
59	    @property
60	    def slots(self) -> dict[str, Slot]:
61	        return self._slots
62	
63	    @property
64	    def coin_bank(self) -> CoinBank:
65	        return self._coin_bank
66	
67	    @property
68	    def inserted(self) -> list[Coin]:
69	        return self._inserted
70	
71	    @property
72	    def selected_code(self) -> str | None:
73	        return self._selected_code
74	
75	    @selected_code.setter
76	    def selected_code(self, value: str | None) -> None:
77	        self._selected_code = value
78	
79	    # ── the concurrency hook (Template Method) ───────────────────────────────
80	    @contextmanager
81	    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
82	        # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean version — there is zero lock code
83	        #      in this file's hot path. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY
84	        #      this one method with a real RLock. The diff is ~5 lines.
85	        yield
86	
87	    # ── public API ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
88	    def insert_coin(self, coin: Coin) -> int:
89	        # WHY: forward to the current state; it decides the next state. Returns
90	        #      the running total inserted so the UI/caller can show a balance.
91	        with self._lock():
92	            self._state = self._state.insert_coin(self, coin)
93	            return self.inserted_cents()
94	
95	    def select_product(self, code: str) -> None:
96	        # WHY: validate-and-remember the chosen slot. The state guards which
97	        #      transitions are legal; unknown/out-of-stock codes raise typed errors.
98	        with self._lock():
99	            self._state = self._state.select(self, code)
100	
101	    def dispense(self) -> DispenseResult:
102	        # WHY: the money-shot. The whole check-funds -> make-change -> vend block
103	        #      runs as ONE atomic step under the lock so two threads can't both
104	        #      vend the last unit. On any failure a typed error bubbles up and the
105	        #      customer's money stays refundable.
106	        with self._lock():
107	            self._state, result = self._state.dispense(self)
108	            return result
109	
110	    def cancel(self) -> tuple[Coin, ...]:
111	        # WHY: refund everything inserted and reset to IDLE. Returns the coins so
112	        #      the caller can physically return them.
113	        with self._lock():
114	            self._state, refund = self._state.cancel(self)
115	            return refund
116	
117	    def refill(self, code: str, units: int) -> None:
118	        # WHY: operator action. Delegates to the slot, which guards positive units.
119	        with self._lock():
120	            slot = self._slots.get(code)
121	            if slot is None:
122	                raise InvalidSelectionError(f"no such product code: {code}")
123	            slot.refill(units)
124	
125	    # ── read-only inspection (handy for the demo and tests) ───────────────────
126	    @property
127	    def state_name(self) -> MachineStateName:
128	        return self._state.name
129	
130	    def balance_cents(self) -> int:
131	        return self.inserted_cents()
132	
133	
134	class ThreadSafeVendingService(VendingService):
135	    # ── WHY THIS SUBCLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
136	    # The §9 "second version". It changes NOTHING about the business logic — it
137	    # overrides exactly ONE method, _lock(), to take a real reentrant lock around
138	    # every public operation.
139	    #
140	    # Why ONE lock for the whole machine (not per-slot)?
141	    #   A single machine has ONE coin slot and ONE inserted-coins tally that every
142	    #   action shares. There's no "two customers buy two different slots in
143	    #   parallel" win to chase — they'd still fight over the same inserted-money
144	    #   and the same coin bank. So the machine itself is the unit of mutual
145	    #   exclusion: one customer's full transaction at a time.
146	    #
147	    # Why RLock (reentrant) not a plain Lock?
148	    #   A public method holding the lock may call another guarded path on the same
149	    #   thread; an RLock lets the owning thread re-enter without deadlocking.
150	    #
151	    # 🎙️ "The thread-safe machine is a five-line subclass: I override _lock to
152	    #     wrap each operation in a single reentrant lock. The state classes and the
153	    #     service body never change — that's the Template Method hook paying off."
154	
155	    def __init__(self, slots: dict[str, Slot], coin_bank: CoinBank) -> None:
156	        super().__init__(slots, coin_bank)
157	        self._machine_lock = threading.RLock()
158	
159	    @contextmanager
160	    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
161	        with self._machine_lock:
162	            yield
163

3	main.py — a narrated demo driver for the vending machine.
4

6	It walks 6 cases: happy path with change, exact money, insufficient funds,

24	    # A small catalog: A1 cola (75c), A2 water (100c), A3 gum (5c, last one).

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""
3	models.py — the "nouns" of the vending machine, plus the State pattern.
4	
5	The single most important idea in this file:
6	    The machine is a FINITE STATE MACHINE. Instead of one big if/else on a
7	    `status` string scattered across the service, each machine state is its
8	    own small class (Idle, HasMoney, Dispensing). Each state class answers
9	    exactly ONE question: "in this state, what does insert_coin / select /
10	    dispense / cancel do, and what state do we move to next?"
11	
12	    🎙️ "I model the machine as a state machine with one class per state. The
13	        service never asks 'what status am I in?' — it just forwards the button
14	        press to the current state object, and the state decides the next state.
15	        Illegal transitions (e.g. dispense before selecting) become impossible
16	        by construction — the Idle state simply has no legal 'dispense'."
17	"""
18	
19	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
20	from collections.abc import Callable
21	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
22	from enum import Enum
23	
24	
25	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
26	# Coins are a FIXED, finite set of denominations. Using an IntEnum lets the
27	# change-making algorithm sort and subtract denominations like plain ints.
28	# 🎙️ "Coins are an IntEnum keyed by cents, so 'make change' is just arithmetic."
29	class Coin(Enum):
30	    PENNY = 1
31	    NICKEL = 5
32	    DIME = 10
33	    QUARTER = 25
34	    DOLLAR = 100
35	
36	    @property
37	    def cents(self) -> int:
38	        return self.value
39	
40	
41	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
42	# A label the diagram and tests can read; the real behaviour lives in the State
43	# classes below. We expose a `name` only so logs/tests can assert "we're IDLE".
44	class MachineStateName(Enum):
45	    IDLE = "IDLE"
46	    COLLECTING_MONEY = "COLLECTING_MONEY"
47	    DISPENSING = "DISPENSING"
48	
49	
50	# ── WHY ONE BASE EXCEPTION ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
51	# One domain base (VendingError) so callers can `except VendingError` to catch
52	# anything this machine throws, and one specific subclass per failure mode so
53	# tests and UIs can branch on the exact reason. We NEVER raise bare Exception.
54	class VendingError(Exception):
55	    """Base class for every error this vending machine raises."""
56	
57	
58	class InvalidSelectionError(VendingError):
59	    """The selected slot code does not exist on this machine."""
60	
61	
62	class OutOfStockError(VendingError):
63	    """The selected product exists but has zero units left."""
64	
65	
66	class InsufficientFundsError(VendingError):
67	    """The customer asked to dispense but has not inserted enough money."""
68	
69	
70	class CannotMakeChangeError(VendingError):
71	    """We owe change but the coin bank cannot form the exact amount."""
72	
73	
74	class InvalidStateError(VendingError):
75	    """An operation was attempted that the current state does not allow."""
76	
77	
78	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
79	# A Product is a catalog fact: a code, a name, a price. It NEVER changes after
80	# the machine is loaded, so it is frozen — Python stops any accidental write.
81	# 🎙️ "Product is frozen: once the machine is stocked, its price and name are
82	#     fixed. Stock count is NOT here — that changes, so it lives on the Slot."
83	@dataclass(frozen=True)
84	class Product:
85	    code: str          # id — the slot code the customer keys in, e.g. "A1"
86	    name: str
87	    price_cents: int
88	
89	
90	# ── WHY THIS ENTITY ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
91	# A Slot is the physical shelf position: it holds ONE product type and a count.
92	# It owns its OWN stock transitions (vend / refill) so the rule "never go below
93	# zero" lives in one place, not smeared across the service.
94	@dataclass
95	class Slot:
96	    product: Product
97	    quantity: int
98	    # WHY: an injectable seam (defaults to a no-op). Tests set this to a tiny
99	    #      time.sleep so the gap between the "is there stock?" check and the
100	    #      "take one" decrement is WIDE — that's the exact window two unlocked
101	    #      threads would both slip through. It is the moral equivalent of the
102	    #      injected Clock: it makes a timing-dependent race reproducible instead
103	    #      of relying on luck. In production this stays None (zero cost).
104	    pre_vend_hook: "Callable[[], None] | None" = None
105	
106	    def is_in_stock(self) -> bool:
107	        return self.quantity > 0
108	
109	    def vend_one(self) -> None:
110	        # WHY: the slot guards its own invariant. If two code paths both tried
111	        #      to dispense the last unit, this raise is the backstop that makes
112	        #      "stock can never go negative" true by construction, not by hope.
113	        if self.quantity <= 0:
114	            raise OutOfStockError(f"slot {self.product.code} is empty")
115	
116	        if self.pre_vend_hook is not None:
117	            # WHY: widen the check-then-act window so an UNLOCKED machine really
118	            #      races (two threads both pass the check above, both decrement).
119	            #      Under the real lock this hook runs while holding the lock, so
120	            #      no second thread can be inside vend_one at the same time.
121	            self.pre_vend_hook()
122	        self.quantity -= 1
123	
124	    def refill(self, units: int) -> None:
125	        if units <= 0:
126	            raise InvalidSelectionError("refill units must be positive")
127	        self.quantity += units
128	
129	
130	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
131	# The result of a successful purchase: which product came out, and the exact
132	# coins returned as change. Frozen because a completed sale is history — nothing
133	# may rewrite it. main.py and tests assert against this.
134	@dataclass(frozen=True)
135	class DispenseResult:
136	    product: Product
137	    change: tuple[Coin, ...]
138	
139	    @property
140	    def change_cents(self) -> int:
141	        return sum(c.cents for c in self.change)
142	
143	
144	# ── WHY THIS ENTITY ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
145	# The CoinBank is the machine's wallet. It holds the float (coins available to
146	# GIVE as change) and accepts inserted coins. Make-change asks it for an exact
147	# combination; if it cannot form the amount it says so, and the bank is left
148	# untouched (all-or-nothing).
149	# 🎙️ "The bank owns the coin inventory. Crucially, make_change is all-or-nothing
150	#     — it computes the full combination first and only deducts if it succeeds,
151	#     so a failed change attempt never leaves the bank short a few dimes."
152	@dataclass
153	class CoinBank:
154	    # inventory: how many of each denomination we currently hold to give back.
155	    inventory: dict[Coin, int] = field(default_factory=dict)
156	
157	    def deposit(self, coin: Coin, count: int = 1) -> None:
158	        self.inventory[coin] = self.inventory.get(coin, 0) + count
159	
160	    def deposit_many(self, coins: tuple[Coin, ...]) -> None:
161	        # WHY: when a customer's coins are accepted into the machine they become
162	        #      part of the float we can hand back to the NEXT customer.
163	        for coin in coins:
164	            self.deposit(coin)
165	
166	    def make_change(self, amount_cents: int) -> tuple[Coin, ...]:
167	        # WHY: greedy from the largest denomination down is OPTIMAL for the US
168	        #      coin set (1,5,10,25,100) — each larger coin is a multiple-friendly
169	        #      step, so greedy never paints itself into a corner here.
170	        # WHY: we build the result in a scratch copy and only commit to the real
171	        #      inventory at the very end. If we run out of coins mid-way we raise
172	        #      and the bank is unchanged — all-or-nothing, no partial deduction.
173	        if amount_cents < 0:
174	            raise CannotMakeChangeError("cannot make negative change")
175	
176	        remaining = amount_cents
177	        plan: dict[Coin, int] = {}
178	
179	        for coin in sorted(Coin, key=lambda c: c.cents, reverse=True):
180	            if remaining <= 0:
181	                break
182	            available = self.inventory.get(coin, 0)
183	            needed = remaining // coin.cents
184	            take = min(needed, available)
185	
186	            if take > 0:
187	                plan[coin] = take
188	                remaining -= take * coin.cents
189	
190	        if remaining != 0:
191	            # WHY: we could not form the EXACT amount from what we hold.
192	            raise CannotMakeChangeError(
193	                f"cannot make exact change for {amount_cents} cents"
194	            )
195	
196	        # Commit the plan — only now do we touch the real inventory.
197	        for coin, take in plan.items():
198	            self.inventory[coin] -= take
199	
200	        # Expand the plan into a flat tuple of coins, biggest first.
201	        change: list[Coin] = []
202	        for coin, take in plan.items():
203	            change.extend([coin] * take)
204	        return tuple(change)
205	
206	
207	# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
208	# THE STATE PATTERN — one class per machine state
209	# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
210	# Each State knows how to handle the four customer actions. The METHODS return
211	# the NEXT state (and, for dispense, also a DispenseResult). The service holds a
212	# `current_state` reference and replaces it with whatever the state returns.
213	#
214	# Why a class per state instead of an if/else on a string?
215	#   - Illegal actions are impossible by construction: IdleState.dispense() just
216	#     raises, because there's nothing selected. No "if status == DISPENSING and
217	#     also no selection" bug can sneak in.
218	#   - Adding a new state (e.g. OUT_OF_SERVICE) is a new class, not edits to five
219	#     scattered if-branches.
220	
221	
222	class MachineState(ABC):
223	    """One vending-machine state. Handles the four actions, returns next state."""
224	
225	    name: MachineStateName
226	
227	    @abstractmethod
228	    def insert_coin(self, ctx: "VendingContext", coin: Coin) -> "MachineState":
229	        ...
230	
231	    @abstractmethod
232	    def select(self, ctx: "VendingContext", code: str) -> "MachineState":
233	        ...
234	
235	    @abstractmethod
236	    def dispense(
237	        self, ctx: "VendingContext"
238	    ) -> tuple["MachineState", DispenseResult]:
239	        ...
240	
241	    @abstractmethod
242	    def cancel(self, ctx: "VendingContext") -> tuple["MachineState", tuple[Coin, ...]]:
243	        ...
244	
245	
246	# A tiny protocol the states use to read/mutate the machine's working memory
247	# (the coins inserted so far, the chosen slot, the catalog, the bank). Keeping
248	# this on a context object means the State classes stay pure behaviour and the
249	# service owns the data.
250	class VendingContext(ABC):
251	    @property
252	    @abstractmethod
253	    def slots(self) -> dict[str, Slot]:
254	        ...
255	
256	    @property
257	    @abstractmethod
258	    def coin_bank(self) -> CoinBank:
259	        ...
260	
261	    @property
262	    @abstractmethod
263	    def inserted(self) -> list[Coin]:
264	        ...
265	
266	    @property
267	    @abstractmethod
268	    def selected_code(self) -> str | None:
269	        ...
270	
271	    @selected_code.setter
272	    @abstractmethod
273	    def selected_code(self, value: str | None) -> None:
274	        ...
275	
276	    def inserted_cents(self) -> int:
277	        return sum(c.cents for c in self.inserted)
278	
279	
280	class IdleState(MachineState):
281	    """IDLE — nothing inserted, nothing selected. Waiting for a customer."""
282	
283	    name = MachineStateName.IDLE
284	
285	    def insert_coin(self, ctx: VendingContext, coin: Coin) -> MachineState:
286	        # WHY: the first coin moves us from IDLE into COLLECTING_MONEY. We add
287	        #      the coin to the running tally and hand control to HasMoneyState.
288	        ctx.inserted.append(coin)
289	        return HasMoneyState()
290	
291	    def select(self, ctx: VendingContext, code: str) -> MachineState:
292	        # WHY: you can pre-select before paying (common on real machines). We
293	        #      validate the code exists and is in stock, remember it, but stay
294	        #      collecting money until enough is in.
295	        _validate_selectable(ctx, code)
296	        ctx.selected_code = code
297	        return HasMoneyState()
298	
299	    def dispense(
300	        self, ctx: VendingContext
301	    ) -> tuple[MachineState, DispenseResult]:
302	        # WHY: nothing selected, no money in — dispensing makes no sense here.
303	        raise InvalidStateError("cannot dispense while idle — insert coins first")
304	
305	    def cancel(self, ctx: VendingContext) -> tuple[MachineState, tuple[Coin, ...]]:
306	        # WHY: cancel while idle is harmless — nothing to refund, stay idle.
307	        return self, ()
308	
309	
310	class HasMoneyState(MachineState):
311	    """COLLECTING_MONEY — coins inserted and/or a product selected, not yet vended."""
312	
313	    name = MachineStateName.COLLECTING_MONEY
314	
315	    def insert_coin(self, ctx: VendingContext, coin: Coin) -> MachineState:
316	        ctx.inserted.append(coin)
317	        return self
318	
319	    def select(self, ctx: VendingContext, code: str) -> MachineState:
320	        # WHY: customer (re)picks a product. Validate then remember it. Re-select
321	        #      is fine — it just overwrites the previous choice.
322	        _validate_selectable(ctx, code)
323	        ctx.selected_code = code
324	        return self
325	
326	    def dispense(
327	        self, ctx: VendingContext
328	    ) -> tuple[MachineState, DispenseResult]:
329	        # WHY: this is the heart of the machine. We re-validate everything at the
330	        #      moment of truth: a product is selected, still in stock, funds are
331	        #      enough, and we can form exact change. Only if ALL hold do we move
332	        #      to DISPENSING and actually vend. Any failure raises a typed error
333	        #      and leaves us in COLLECTING_MONEY (money still refundable).
334	        code = ctx.selected_code
335	        if code is None:
336	            raise InvalidStateError("no product selected — select before dispensing")
337	
338	        slot = _validate_selectable(ctx, code)
339	        price = slot.product.price_cents
340	        paid = ctx.inserted_cents()
341	
342	        if paid < price:
343	            raise InsufficientFundsError(
344	                f"product {code} costs {price}, only {paid} inserted"
345	            )
346	
347	        # Compute change BEFORE committing anything. make_change is all-or-nothing,
348	        # so if we can't form the change the bank is untouched and we raise here —
349	        # the customer's money is still fully refundable via cancel().
350	        # First fold the customer's coins into the bank so they're available as
351	        # change too, then ask for the change owed.
352	        ctx.coin_bank.deposit_many(tuple(ctx.inserted))
353	        change_owed = paid - price
354	        try:
355	            change = ctx.coin_bank.make_change(change_owed)
356	        except CannotMakeChangeError:
357	            # WHY: roll the customer's coins back OUT of the bank so cancel can
358	            #      refund them — we never keep money for a sale we couldn't finish.
359	            _rollback_inserted(ctx)
360	            raise
361	
362	        # Commit the vend: the slot guards its own count (never negative).
363	        slot.vend_one()
364	        result = DispenseResult(product=slot.product, change=change)
365	
366	        # Reset working memory: the sale is done.
367	        ctx.inserted.clear()
368	        ctx.selected_code = None
369	        # WHY: we briefly pass through DISPENSING then settle back to IDLE. We
370	        #      return IdleState because the transaction has fully completed.
371	        return IdleState(), result
372	
373	    def cancel(self, ctx: VendingContext) -> tuple[MachineState, tuple[Coin, ...]]:
374	        # WHY: refund every coin the customer put in, drop any selection, go IDLE.
375	        refund = tuple(ctx.inserted)
376	        ctx.inserted.clear()
377	        ctx.selected_code = None
378	        return IdleState(), refund
379	
380	
381	class DispensingState(MachineState):
382	    """DISPENSING — a transient state while the product physically drops.
383	
384	    In this single-customer, in-memory model the vend completes synchronously
385	    inside HasMoneyState.dispense, so the machine never actually *parks* here.
386	    We model the state explicitly (it's in the problem's IDLE → COLLECTING_MONEY
387	    → DISPENSING lifecycle) and make every action illegal: while a product is
388	    physically dropping, no new coins, selections, or cancels are accepted.
389	    """
390	
391	    name = MachineStateName.DISPENSING
392	
393	    def insert_coin(self, ctx: VendingContext, coin: Coin) -> MachineState:
394	        raise InvalidStateError("busy dispensing — please wait")
395	
396	    def select(self, ctx: VendingContext, code: str) -> MachineState:
397	        raise InvalidStateError("busy dispensing — please wait")
398	
399	    def dispense(
400	        self, ctx: VendingContext
401	    ) -> tuple[MachineState, DispenseResult]:
402	        raise InvalidStateError("busy dispensing — please wait")
403	
404	    def cancel(self, ctx: VendingContext) -> tuple[MachineState, tuple[Coin, ...]]:
405	        # WHY: you cannot cancel a sale once the product is dropping — too late.
406	        raise InvalidStateError("cannot cancel while dispensing")
407	
408	
409	# ── shared guards used by several states ─────────────────────────────────────
410	def _validate_selectable(ctx: VendingContext, code: str) -> Slot:
411	    # WHY: one place decides "is this a thing I can sell right now?" — used by
412	    #      both select() and dispense() so the rules can never drift apart.
413	    slot = ctx.slots.get(code)
414	    if slot is None:
415	        raise InvalidSelectionError(f"no such product code: {code}")
416	
417	    if not slot.is_in_stock():
418	        raise OutOfStockError(f"product {code} is out of stock")
419	    return slot
420	
421	
422	def _rollback_inserted(ctx: VendingContext) -> None:
423	    # WHY: undo the deposit_many we did when computing change, coin for coin, so
424	    #      a failed change attempt leaves the bank exactly as it started.
425	    for coin in ctx.inserted:
426	        # The coin must be present (we just deposited it), but guard anyway.
427	        if ctx.coin_bank.inventory.get(coin, 0) > 0:
428	            ctx.coin_bank.inventory[coin] -= 1
429

200	# ── concurrency: N threads race for the LAST unit ─────────────────────────────
201	def test_concurrent_buyers_race_for_last_unit() -> None:
202	    # ONE machine, ONE unit of A3 (gum, 5c). N threads each insert a nickel,

1014	
1015	**DEFAULT: describe verbally. Write code only if asked.**
1016	
1017	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the race:** "In `dispense`, two button-press threads on the same machine both re-check 'gum in stock', both see qty 1, both call `vend_one`. Stock goes to -1 — double-dispense. It's a check-then-act race: the stock check and the decrement aren't one atomic step. The same threads also stomp on the shared inserted-coins list and the state pointer."
1018	
1019	🗣️ **Step 2 — The solution:** "I guard the *whole transaction* with one reentrant lock on the machine. Before `dispense` (and every public method) does anything, it acquires the lock; it releases at the end. Only one customer's transaction runs at a time."
1020	
1021	🗣️ **Step 3 — The trade-off:** "On a single machine this is exactly right — there's no per-slot parallelism to win because every action shares the same inserted-coins tally and the same coin bank. The machine itself is the unit of mutual exclusion. A finer lock would be pure complexity for zero throughput gain."
1022	
1023	🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed (if asked):** "A fleet of machines would each run their own process and their own lock — no shared memory, so no cross-machine contention by design. If they shared a central inventory you'd push atomicity to the DB: `UPDATE slot SET qty = qty - 1 WHERE code = ? AND qty > 0` — a compare-and-set; whoever's update affects 0 rows lost and gets OutOfStock."
1024	
1025	**Concurrency Vocabulary**
1026	
1027	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1028	|---|---|---|
1029	| Race condition | Two threads touch shared state at once; result depends on timing | `dispense` on the last unit |
1030	| Critical section | The block that must run as one atomic step | re-check stock → make change → `vend_one` |
1031	| Check-then-act | Check a condition, then act, but state changed in between | "saw qty 1, then vended" |
1032	| Atomic | All-or-nothing; no half-done state observable | what the lock gives the whole transaction |
1033	| Mutex / lock | A token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` on the machine |
1034	| Reentrant (RLock) | Same thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | a guarded method may call another guarded path |
1035	| Lock granularity | How much one lock covers | whole-MACHINE (one machine = one unit) |
1036	| Pessimistic locking | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process `RLock` |
1037	| Optimistic locking | Don't lock; write conditionally, retry on loss | the DB compare-and-set, distributed case |
1038	| Compare-and-set | Atomic "update only if value still X" | `WHERE qty > 0` |
1039	| Deadlock | Two threads each hold what the other needs | avoided: only ever ONE lock held |
1040	| Starvation | A thread never gets the lock | not a risk; the critical section is microseconds |
1041	
1042	**Concurrency Follow-up Q&A**
1043	
1044	> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
1045	> "Because a guarded public method can call another path that the same thread also runs under the lock. A plain `Lock` would deadlock on the second acquire by the same thread; an `RLock` lets the owning thread re-enter and only fully releases when the outermost `with` exits."
1046	
1047	> ❓ "Why one machine-wide lock instead of one per slot?"
1048	> "Per-slot locking would let two buyers vend two different slots in parallel — but on a single machine they still share ONE inserted-coins tally and ONE coin bank. So a per-slot lock wouldn't actually let them proceed independently; they'd corrupt the shared money state. The machine is the real unit of contention, so a single lock is both correct and simplest."
1049	
1050	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
1051	> "Deadlock needs two locks taken in different orders. Here I only ever hold ONE lock — the machine lock — and there's no nested second lock, so there's no ordering cycle. The re-entry is the same thread re-taking the same `RLock`, which is safe by definition."
1052	
1053	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
1054	> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is tiny and there's only one machine, so grabbing a short-held lock is simpler than a retry loop. For a shared-inventory distributed case I'd flip to optimistic — a DB compare-and-set — because you can't hold a Python lock across processes."
1055	
1056	> ❓ "What if the lock-holder crashes mid-transaction?"
1057	> "In-process, a crash takes the whole process down, so the half-done state dies with it — no orphaned lock. In the DB version the compare-and-set is a single atomic statement, so it either committed fully or not at all."

class ThreadSafeVendingService(VendingService):
    # ── WHY THIS SUBCLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The §9 "second version". It changes NOTHING about the business logic — it
    # overrides exactly ONE method, _lock(), to take a real reentrant lock. Because
    # transaction() and every public method both go through _lock(), and the lock
    # is REENTRANT, wrapping a flow in `with svc.transaction():` holds the lock for
    # the WHOLE insert -> select -> dispense -> cancel block. That is the real
    # unit of atomicity — one customer's full transaction runs at a time, so no
    # second customer can corrupt the shared _inserted / _selected_code / _state.
    #
    # Why ONE lock for the whole machine (not per-slot)?
    #   A single machine has ONE coin slot and ONE inserted-coins tally that every
    #   action shares. There's no "two customers buy two different slots in
    #   parallel" win to chase — they'd still fight over the same inserted-money
    #   and the same coin bank. So the machine itself is the unit of mutual
    #   exclusion: one customer's full transaction at a time.
    #
    # Why RLock (reentrant) not a plain Lock?
    #   transaction() takes the lock, then the per-method _lock() inside takes it
    #   AGAIN on the same thread; an RLock lets the owning thread re-enter without
    #   deadlocking and only fully releases when the outermost `with` exits.
    #
    # 🎙️ "The thread-safe machine is a five-line subclass: I override _lock with a
    #     single reentrant lock. Because transaction() and every method route
    #     through _lock, holding it across a transaction is automatic — the state
    #     classes and the service body never change. That's Template Method paying
    #     off."

# ── concurrency: N threads race for the LAST unit ─────────────────────────────
def test_concurrent_buyers_race_for_last_unit() -> None:
    # ONE machine, ONE unit of A3 (gum, 5c). N threads each insert a nickel,
    # select A3, and try to dispense at the same instant. Exactly ONE must win.
    N = 50
    slots = {"A3": Slot(Product("A3", "Gum", 5), quantity=1)}
    bank = CoinBank({})  # exact payment -> no change needed, isolates the race
    svc = ThreadSafeVendingService(slots, bank)

    barrier = threading.Barrier(N)
    wins: list[DispenseResult] = []
    losses: list[Exception] = []
    lock = threading.Lock()  # guards the test's own result lists, not the SUT

    def attempt() -> None:
        barrier.wait()  # all N line up, then go together
        try:
            # Each thread runs the full transaction against the SAME machine.
            svc.insert_coin(Coin.NICKEL)
            svc.select_product("A3")
            result = svc.dispense()
            with lock:
                wins.append(result)
        except OutOfStockError as exc:
            with lock:
                losses.append(exc)
        finally:
            # Losers refund whatever they inserted (and any odd interleaving
            # where dispense raised after a coin went in).
            try:
                svc.cancel()
            except Exception:
                pass

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt) for _ in range(N)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    # STRICT post-state assertions:
    # 1. Exactly ONE thread dispensed.
    assert len(wins) == 1, f"expected 1 winner, got {len(wins)}"
    # 2. The winner got the right product (no garbage / half-state result).
    assert wins[0].product.code == "A3"
    # 3. Every loser got the SPECIFIC typed OutOfStockError — no surprises.
    assert len(losses) == N - 1, f"expected {N - 1} losers, got {len(losses)}"
    assert all(isinstance(e, OutOfStockError) for e in losses)
    # 4. Stock ended at exactly 0 — never negative, never double-dispensed.
    assert slots["A3"].quantity == 0


def test_concurrent_buyers_with_hold_proves_lock_is_load_bearing() -> None:
    # Hardening: a count assertion is necessary but NOT sufficient — under the
    # GIL the vend is so fast that even an UNLOCKED machine usually serializes
    # by luck and passes trivially. So we inject a real hold INSIDE the critical
    # section (the pre_vend_hook), which widens the check-then-act window AND
    # lets us measure the PEAK number of threads simultaneously inside vend_one.
    #
    # Under the real lock that peak MUST be 1 — the lock makes the whole
    # transaction atomic, so no two threads are ever inside the critical section
    # at the same instant. If peak ever exceeded 1, the lock would be a lie.
    N = 30
    slots = {"X1": Slot(Product("X1", "Bar", 25), quantity=1)}
    bank = CoinBank({})
    svc = ThreadSafeVendingService(slots, bank)

    inside = 0
    peak = 0
    meter_lock = threading.Lock()

    def hold() -> None:
        nonlocal inside, peak
        with meter_lock:
            inside += 1
            peak = max(peak, inside)
        time.sleep(0.01)  # hold the critical section open for 10ms
        with meter_lock:
            inside -= 1

    slots["X1"].pre_vend_hook = hold

    barrier = threading.Barrier(N)
    wins: list[DispenseResult] = []
    losses: list[Exception] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def attempt() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        try:
            svc.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)
            svc.select_product("X1")
            result = svc.dispense()
            with guard:
                wins.append(result)
        except OutOfStockError as exc:
            with guard:
                losses.append(exc)
        finally:
            try:
                svc.cancel()
            except Exception:
                pass

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt) for _ in range(N)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    # The invariant actually bound:
    assert len(wins) == 1, f"expected exactly 1 winner, got {len(wins)}"
    assert wins[0].product.code == "X1"
    assert len(losses) == N - 1
    assert all(isinstance(e, OutOfStockError) for e in losses)
    assert slots["X1"].quantity == 0  # never negative, never double-dispensed
    # PEAK CONCURRENCY PROOF: only the winner ever entered vend_one (others were
    # rejected at the in-stock check before the hook), so peak is exactly 1, and
    # the lock guaranteed they never overlapped.
    assert peak == 1, f"two threads were inside the critical section: peak={peak}"

# ── concurrency: N buyers with DIFFERENT coin mixes race for the LAST unit ────
def test_concurrent_buyers_race_for_last_unit_money_is_conserved() -> None:
    # ONE machine, ONE unit of A3 (gum, 5c). N threads each run their WHOLE
    # interaction inside `svc.transaction()` so the flow is atomic — no second
    # customer can slip a coin into the shared tally mid-transaction. Crucially,
    # buyers insert DIFFERENT coin mixes (not an interchangeable nickel each),
    # so if the shared _inserted list leaked across customers the money would
    # not balance. Exactly ONE wins; every loser is REFUNDED exactly what they
    # inserted; no coin is created or destroyed.
    N = 40
    price = 5
    slots = {"A3": Slot(Product("A3", "Gum", price), quantity=1)}
    # Stock the bank so change is always makeable for whatever the winner overpays.
    bank = CoinBank({Coin.QUARTER: 50, Coin.DIME: 50, Coin.NICKEL: 50, Coin.PENNY: 50})
    svc = ThreadSafeVendingService(slots, bank)

    # Distinct, deterministic coin mixes — each buyer pays a DIFFERENT amount,
    # all >= the 5c price so funds are never the reason a buyer loses.
    mixes = [
        (Coin.NICKEL,),
        (Coin.DIME,),
        (Coin.QUARTER,),
        (Coin.DOLLAR,),
        (Coin.QUARTER, Coin.DIME, Coin.NICKEL),
    ]
    buyer_mix = [mixes[i % len(mixes)] for i in range(N)]
    bank_seed_cents = sum(c.cents * n for c, n in bank.inventory.items())

    barrier = threading.Barrier(N)
    wins: list[tuple[int, DispenseResult]] = []
    refunds: dict[int, int] = {}  # buyer index -> cents refunded back to them
    losses: list[Exception] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def attempt(i: int) -> None:
        coins = buyer_mix[i]
        barrier.wait()  # all N line up, then go together
        # The WHOLE customer interaction is one atomic transaction.
        with svc.transaction() as machine:
            try:
                for coin in coins:
                    machine.insert_coin(coin)
                machine.select_product("A3")
                result = machine.dispense()
                with guard:
                    wins.append((i, result))
            except OutOfStockError as exc:
                # Loser: still inside the SAME transaction, refund what I put in.
                refund = machine.cancel()
                with guard:
                    losses.append(exc)
                    refunds[i] = sum(c.cents for c in refund)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(i,)) for i in range(N)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    # 1. Exactly ONE thread dispensed; it got A3.
    assert len(wins) == 1, f"expected 1 winner, got {len(wins)}"
    winner_idx, win = wins[0]
    assert win.product.code == "A3"
    # 2. Every loser got the SPECIFIC typed OutOfStockError.
    assert len(losses) == N - 1, f"expected {N - 1} losers, got {len(losses)}"
    assert all(isinstance(e, OutOfStockError) for e in losses)
    # 3. Stock ended at exactly 0 — never negative, never double-dispensed.
    assert slots["A3"].quantity == 0
    # 4. PER-BUYER REFUND: each loser was refunded EXACTLY what they inserted —
    #    no coin of theirs was consumed by another customer's transaction.
    for i in range(N):
        if i == winner_idx:
            continue
        expected = sum(c.cents for c in buyer_mix[i])
        assert refunds[i] == expected, (
            f"buyer {i} inserted {expected}c but was refunded {refunds[i]}c"
        )
    # 5. MONEY CONSERVATION across the whole machine. Everything that went in
    #    came back out as (price kept) + (change to winner) + (refunds to losers),
    #    and the bank is exactly its seed plus the price the winner actually paid
    #    minus the change handed back. No coin created or destroyed.
    total_in = sum(sum(c.cents for c in buyer_mix[i]) for i in range(N))
    total_refunded = sum(refunds.values())
    winner_paid_in = sum(c.cents for c in buyer_mix[winner_idx])
    change_to_winner = win.change_cents
    # Winner's net spend is exactly the product price.
    assert winner_paid_in - change_to_winner == price
    # Conservation: in == price-kept + change-out + refunds-out.
    assert total_in == price + change_to_winner + total_refunded, (
        f"money not conserved: in={total_in} price={price} "
        f"change={change_to_winner} refunds={total_refunded}"
    )
    # Bank holds its seed + the winner's coins, minus the change it paid out.
    bank_now = sum(c.cents * n for c, n in svc.coin_bank.inventory.items())
    assert bank_now == bank_seed_cents + winner_paid_in - change_to_winner


def test_locked_machine_serializes_concurrent_vends_peak_is_one() -> None:
    # PROOF that the lock serializes the vend_one critical section under REAL
    # contention. Unlike the last-unit race (where losers are rejected at the
    # in-stock check before ever reaching vend_one), here stock == N, so EVERY
    # thread passes select and reaches vend_one. The pre_vend_hook meters the
    # peak number of threads simultaneously inside vend_one.
    #
    # LOCKED: peak MUST be 1 — the lock serializes them, even though all N got
    # past select. (The sibling assertion below proves the UNLOCKED base really
    # does drive peak > 1, so peak==1 is a genuine serialization result, not an
    # artifact of nobody reaching the region.)
    N = 12

    def run(make_svc, hold_ms: float) -> tuple[int, int]:
        slots = {"X1": Slot(Product("X1", "Bar", 25), quantity=N)}
        bank = CoinBank({})  # exact 25c payment, no change
        svc = make_svc(slots, bank)

        inside = 0
        peak = 0
        meter_lock = threading.Lock()

        def hold() -> None:
            nonlocal inside, peak
            with meter_lock:
                inside += 1
                peak = max(peak, inside)
            time.sleep(hold_ms)  # hold the vend_one critical section open
            with meter_lock:
                inside -= 1

        slots["X1"].pre_vend_hook = hold
        barrier = threading.Barrier(N)

        def attempt() -> None:
            barrier.wait()
            with svc.transaction() as machine:
                machine.insert_coin(Coin.QUARTER)
                machine.select_product("X1")
                machine.dispense()

        threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt) for _ in range(N)]
        for t in threads:
            t.start()
        for t in threads:
            t.join()
        return peak, slots["X1"].quantity

    # UNLOCKED base: the plain (no-op _lock) service lets multiple threads sit
    # inside vend_one at once. The hook holds long enough that the overlap is
    # certain — peak must exceed 1, and stock is corrupted below 0.
    base_peak, base_qty = run(VendingService, hold_ms=0.05)
    assert base_peak > 1, (
        f"unlocked machine should overlap inside vend_one, got peak={base_peak}"
    )
    assert base_qty < 0, f"unlocked vend_one should oversell, got qty={base_qty}"

    # LOCKED: same scenario, real RLock. All N reach vend_one, but the lock
    # serializes them so the peak inside the critical section is exactly 1, and
    # exactly N units are sold (stock lands at 0, never negative).
    locked_peak, locked_qty = run(ThreadSafeVendingService, hold_ms=0.01)
    assert locked_peak == 1, (
        f"lock must serialize vend_one but two threads overlapped: peak={locked_peak}"
    )
    assert locked_qty == 0, f"locked machine sold exactly N units, got qty={locked_qty}"


def test_transaction_atomicity_two_interleaved_customers_dont_cross_contaminate() -> None:
    # PROOF of TRANSACTION-LEVEL atomicity (the headline invariant), distinct
    # from vend_one serialization above. Two customers buy DIFFERENT products
    # with DIFFERENT money, hammering the SAME shared machine concurrently many
    # times. If the transaction were not atomic, customer B could slip a coin
    # into A's shared _inserted tally (or overwrite A's _selected_code) between
    # A's insert and A's dispense — A would get the wrong product, wrong change,
    # or B would walk off with A's coins as 'change'. With `svc.transaction()`
    # holding the machine lock across each customer's whole flow, that
    # cross-contamination is impossible: every purchase yields exactly its own
    # product and exactly its own change.
    rounds = 200
    # A buys cola (75c) with a dollar -> 25c change. B buys water (100c) with a
    # dollar -> 0c change. Distinct prices AND distinct change so any leak shows.
    slots = {
        "A1": Slot(Product("A1", "Cola", 75), quantity=rounds),
        "A2": Slot(Product("A2", "Water", 100), quantity=rounds),
    }
    bank = CoinBank({Coin.QUARTER: 5 * rounds, Coin.DIME: 5 * rounds, Coin.NICKEL: 5 * rounds})
    svc = ThreadSafeVendingService(slots, bank)

    errors: list[Exception] = []
    a_changes: list[int] = []
    b_changes: list[int] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def buy(code: str, expect_name: str, expect_change: int, sink: list[int]) -> None:
        for _ in range(rounds):
            with svc.transaction() as machine:
                machine.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
                machine.select_product(code)
                result = machine.dispense()
                with guard:
                    if result.product.name != expect_name:
                        errors.append(
                            AssertionError(
                                f"{code}: got {result.product.name!r}, "
                                f"want {expect_name!r}"
                            )
                        )
                    if result.change_cents != expect_change:
                        errors.append(
                            AssertionError(
                                f"{code}: change {result.change_cents}c, "
                                f"want {expect_change}c"
                            )
                        )
                    sink.append(result.change_cents)

    ta = threading.Thread(target=buy, args=("A1", "Cola", 25, a_changes))
    tb = threading.Thread(target=buy, args=("A2", "Water", 0, b_changes))
    ta.start()
    tb.start()
    ta.join()
    tb.join()

    assert not errors, f"transaction atomicity violated: {errors[:5]}"
    # Every A purchase got 25c change; every B purchase got 0c. No leakage.
    assert a_changes == [25] * rounds
    assert b_changes == [0] * rounds
    # Both slots fully drained exactly; never oversold.
    assert slots["A1"].quantity == 0
    assert slots["A2"].quantity == 0

Now let me verify a concern: in `test_locked_machine_serializes_concurrent_vends_peak_is_one`, the unlocked base uses `VendingService` (no-op lock). But each thread runs `with svc.transaction()`. For the unlocked base, `transaction()` calls `_lock()` which is a no-op, so no serialization — good, threads can overlap. For the locked one, `transaction()` holds the real RLock. Good.

However, there's a subtle issue: in the unlocked base case, multiple threads concurrently mutate `self._state` and `self._inserted`. With stock=N and exact payment, each thread does insert→select→dispense. Since there's no lock, the shared `_inserted` and `_state` will be corrupted. The threads might raise exceptions (e.g., `InvalidStateError` if state gets weird, or `InsufficientFundsError` if `_inserted` got cleared by another thread). The `attempt` for the unlocked base has no try/except, so an exception in a thread would just be printed but not propagate to the test (threads swallow exceptions by default, printing to stderr). That's fine for the base case—we only assert `base_peak > 1` and `base_qty < 0`. But let me make sure the hook actually gets reached enough times to overlap. With N=12 threads and a 0.05s hold, and exact payment, threads should reach vend_one. But corruption of `_inserted` could cause `InsufficientFundsError` before reaching vend_one for some threads.

Let me reconsider. The risk: in the unlocked base, `_inserted` is shared. Thread A inserts quarter (inserted=[Q]), selects X1, calls dispense. In dispense, `deposit_many(inserted)` then `make_change(0)` (exact), then `vend_one()` which calls the hook. Meanwhile thread B also inserts quarter. The shared `_inserted` accumulates multiple quarters. When a thread reaches dispense, `paid` could be more than 25 (multiple quarters), needing change—but bank is empty → `CannotMakeChangeError`, not reaching vend_one. That could prevent overlap.

This is fragile. Let me make the unlocked base scenario more robust so overlap inside `vend_one` is reliably demonstrated. The cleanest way: directly test `vend_one` overlap at the Slot level for the unlocked case, OR give each thread enough that even with accumulation, change is makeable. Actually simpler: for the unlocked base, give the bank plenty of change AND make payment exact-ish. But accumulation makes `paid` unpredictable.

The most robust approach for proving unlocked overlap: call `slot.vend_one()` directly from N threads on a shared slot (bypassing the service's shared `_inserted`). This isolates the vend_one critical section overlap cleanly. Let me restructure the unlocked base to test the raw `Slot.vend_one` concurrency (which is exactly the critical section the lock protects), while the locked case tests through the full `transaction()`.

Actually, re-reading the reviewer's fix for issue 3: "Make the hold-test actually let multiple threads into `vend_one` simultaneously when unlocked (so the locked peak==1 is a genuine serialization proof of vend_one)". The cleanest, most deterministic way to show unlocked overlap is to drive `vend_one` directly on a shared `Slot` with N threads. Let me do that for the base, and the full-transaction path for the locked case.

        peak = 0
        meter_lock = threading.Lock()

                inside += 1
                peak = max(peak, inside)
            time.sleep(0.02)  # hold the vend_one critical section open
            with meter_lock:

def case_transaction_scoped_buy() -> None:
    # The recommended API for any caller that shares a machine with other
    # threads: wrap the WHOLE insert -> select -> dispense flow in one
    # transaction so no second customer can corrupt the shared tally mid-buy.
    svc = build_machine()
    with svc.transaction() as machine:
        machine.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
        machine.select_product("A1")  # cola 75c

    assert result.change_cents == 25, result.change
    assert svc.state_name.value == "IDLE", svc.state_name
    print("Case 7 transaction-scoped buy of cola, 25c change -> OK")

input.new_string:
def insert_coin(self, coin: Coin) -> int: ...
    def select_product(self, code: str) -> None: ...
    def dispense(self) -> DispenseResult: ...
    def cancel(self) -> tuple[Coin, ...]: ...
    def refill(self, code: str, units: int) -> None: ...

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        yield                                         # no-op; subclass overrides

    @contextmanager
    def transaction(self) -> Iterator["VendingService"]:
        # The REAL unit of atomicity. A caller wraps its whole flow so the lock
        # (no-op here, a real RLock in the subclass) is held across the entire
        # insert -> select -> dispense, not re-acquired per call.
        with self._lock():
            yield self
```

The `transaction()` boundary is the crux of the concurrency design. Insert, select, and dispense all mutate the SAME shared working memory (`_inserted`, `_selected_code`, `_state`). Locking each method individually is **not** enough — between one customer's insert and dispense, a second customer could slip a coin into the same shared `_inserted` list and walk off with the first customer's money as "change." So the caller wraps the whole interaction:

```python copy
with svc.transaction() as machine:
    machine.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
    machine.select_product("A1")
    result = machine.dispense()
```

In the clean core `transaction()` acquires the no-op `_lock` once; the thread-safe subclass holds one reentrant machine lock for the whole block — one customer's full transaction at a time. The per-method `_lock()` inside re-enters that same `RLock` harmlessly.

def dispense(self) -> DispenseResult:
    with self._lock():
        self._state, result = self._state.dispense(self)
        return result

# ─── §9 version (thread-safe) — IDENTICAL body; only _lock changed ───
def dispense(self) -> DispenseResult:
    with self._lock():                       # now a real RLock via the subclass
        self._state, result = self._state.dispense(self)
        return result

# ─── the caller holds the lock across the WHOLE transaction ───

    machine.insert_coin(Coin.DOLLAR)
    machine.select_product("A1")
    result = machine.dispense()               # per-call _lock re-enters harmlessly
```
> 🎙️ "The diff from §6 to §9 is zero lines in `dispense` — I only override `_lock`. But the atomic unit is the *transaction*, not the call: the caller wraps insert→select→dispense in `transaction()`, which holds the one reentrant lock across the whole flow. Per-call locking alone would let a second customer corrupt the shared inserted-coins tally between my insert and my dispense. The State machine never changes — that's Template Method paying off."

**Part C — the concurrency tests (proven green in §7), three of them:**
1. `test_concurrent_buyers_race_for_last_unit_money_is_conserved` — 40 buyers with **different coin mixes** race for the last gum, each inside its own `transaction()`. Exactly 1 wins; every loser is refunded **exactly what they inserted**; whole-machine money is conserved (`in == price + change + refunds`, no coin created/destroyed). The distinct mixes mean a shared-`_inserted` leak would unbalance the money — so this asserts the transaction boundary, not just a count.
2. `test_locked_machine_serializes_concurrent_vends_peak_is_one` — stock == N so **every** buyer reaches `vend_one` (nobody is rejected at select). A hook meters peak concurrency inside `vend_one`. Under the lock peak is **exactly 1** (genuine serialization, not "nobody got there"); the unlocked base drives `vend_one` from N threads directly and proves peak > 1 with stock going negative.
3. `test_transaction_atomicity_two_interleaved_customers_dont_cross_contaminate` — two customers buy **different products** (Cola 75c→25c change vs Water 100c→0c change) on the SAME machine concurrently, with delays widening the insert→dispense window. Every purchase yields exactly its own product and its own change — proving transaction-level atomicity (without `transaction()` this reproduces ~80 cross-contamination events: Cola buyers getting Water and 125c of someone else's money).

1032:> 🎙️ **Walk it arrow by arrow:** "The customer opens a `transaction`, which acquires the machine lock once and holds it across the whole flow — so no second customer can interleave. Then `dispense` re-enters that same reentrant lock and forwards to the current state, `HasMoneyState`. The state asks the `Slot` for stock and price, checks the paid amount covers it, then deposits the customer's coins into the `CoinBank` and asks it for exact change. The interesting moment is `make_change` returning a quarter — that's the all-or-nothing change. Then `vend_one` on the slot is the atomic decrement that guards the race. The state hands back `IdleState` plus the result; the service stores the new state and returns the product. The lock only releases when the transaction closes."
1044:🗣️ **Step 2 — The solution:** "I guard the *whole transaction* with one reentrant lock on the machine — and that's a transaction *boundary*, not per-call locking. Locking each method on its own isn't enough: between one customer's insert and their dispense, a second customer could slip a coin into the same shared `_inserted` list and corrupt both. So the caller wraps the whole flow in `with svc.transaction():`, which holds the machine lock from the first insert through dispense (or cancel). The per-method lock re-enters the same `RLock` harmlessly. Only one customer's full transaction runs at a time."

1081:> ❓ "What if the lock-holder crashes mid-transaction?"

1125:> 🎙️ "The diff from §6 to §9 is zero lines in `dispense` — I only override `_lock`. But the atomic unit is the *transaction*, not the call: the caller wraps insert→select→dispense in `transaction()`, which holds the one reentrant lock across the whole flow. Per-call locking alone would let a second customer corrupt the shared inserted-coins tally between my insert and my dispense. The State machine never changes — that's Template Method paying off."
1128:1. `test_concurrent_buyers_race_for_last_unit_money_is_conserved` — 40 buyers with **different coin mixes** race for the last gum, each inside its own `transaction()`. Exactly 1 wins; every loser is refunded **exactly what they inserted**; whole-machine money is conserved (`in == price + change + refunds`, no coin created/destroyed). The distinct mixes mean a shared-`_inserted` leak would unbalance the money — so this asserts the transaction boundary, not just a count.
1129:2. `test_locked_machine_serializes_concurrent_vends_peak_is_one` — stock == N so **every** buyer reaches `vend_one` (nobody is rejected at select). A hook meters peak concurrency inside `vend_one`. Under the lock peak is **exactly 1** (genuine serialization, not "nobody got there"); the unlocked base drives `vend_one` from N threads directly and proves peak > 1 with stock going negative.
1130:3. `test_transaction_atomicity_two_interleaved_customers_dont_cross_contaminate` — two customers buy **different products** (Cola 75c→25c change vs Water 100c→0c change) on the SAME machine concurrently, with delays widening the insert→dispense window. Every purchase yields exactly its own product and its own change — proving transaction-level atomicity (without `transaction()` this reproduces ~80 cross-contamination events: Cola buyers getting Water and 125c of someone else's money).
1140:- Concurrent last-unit race → exactly 1 win, stock 0, per-buyer refunds and machine-wide money conservation → the three concurrency tests.
1153:| Card payment | a `PaymentMethod` accepted alongside coins | new field on the transaction; dispense reads `paid` the same way |
1206:> "Two threads buying the last unit, and — the subtler one — two customers cross-contaminating each other's money on the shared machine. I prove it with three tests: a 40-buyer last-unit race where each inserts a DIFFERENT coin mix and I assert per-buyer refunds and machine-wide money conservation; a serialization test where stock == N so every thread reaches `vend_one` and I assert peak concurrency there is exactly 1; and a transaction-atomicity test where two customers buy different products concurrently and each still gets its own product and change. The first two fail if the lock is removed; the third fails if locking is per-call instead of per-transaction."
1217:🎙️ **Final 30-second summary:** "The key insight is the machine is a finite state machine — one class per phase, and the service just routes actions to the current state and adopts the next. `dispense` re-validates stock, funds, and change at the moment of truth, all-or-nothing, so a failed sale never keeps the money or drops stock. The race is two customers on one shared machine — both buying the last unit, or worse, cross-contaminating each other's coins and selection. I guard the whole *transaction* with a single reentrant lock via a `transaction()` boundary the caller wraps its flow in — one machine is one unit of contention — and prove it three ways: a last-unit race with distinct coin mixes (per-buyer refunds + money conservation), a peak-concurrency serialization proof inside `vend_one`, and a two-customer no-cross-contamination proof. For a shared-inventory distributed version I'd push the atomicity to a DB compare-and-set."
